revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Iwakura rock]][[fa:ایواکورا]][[ja:磐座]][[ko:이와쿠라]][[ru:Ивакура (синто)]][[simple:Iwakura]]
{{Short description|Shinto sacred rock}} [[File:Yamanokami_Site_(Sakurai),_iwakura.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|{{Center|Yamanokami Site ([[:en:桜井市|Sakurai City]], [[:en:奈良県|Nara Prefecture]])}}]] [[File:Meotoiwa.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|[[Meoto Iwa]] a subtype of Iwakura]] {{Nihongo|'''Iwakura'''|岩倉}} refers to the belief in rocks as {{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}} containing [[Kami]] in [[Ko-Shintō|ancient Shinto]]. It also refers to the rock itself, which is the object of worship.
== Overview == {{ill|Nature worship|en|Nature worship|qid=Q8189650|lt=Nature worship|1=Nature worship}}, also known as spirit worship or {{ill|Animism|en|Animism|qid=Q43004|lt=Animism|1=Animism}}, is a type of base faith that has been present in Japan since ancient times. This form of worship revolves around the belief in gods and spirits that reside in nature. In Shinto rituals, gods are said to have descended from [[shintai]], a rock, and the {{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}, called [[himorogi]], was made the center of the ritual, symbolizing the divine power of the gods.
As time passed and {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=temples|1=Shinto architecture}}, where gods were believed to reside, became more permanent, the object of worship shifted from the body of the gods to the shrine itself. Sacred trees and stones, adorned with {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}} ropes, can still be seen in many temple precincts.
In addition to rocks, there are several other forms of belief in nature in Japan, including [[Chinju no Mori]], the "island" as a forbidden area, the [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]] of [[Munakata Taisha]], belief in mountains such as Rokko Himei Shrine and {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, belief in fire, waterfalls, and various weather phenomena such as wind, rain, and lightning.
There is also mention of another rock-related object called Iwasaka, which is believed to be a ritual site centered on a rock. However, there is little evidence of its existence compared to Iwaza. The ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]]'' (Chronicles of Japan) distinguishes Iwasaka from Iwaza, suggesting that it is a distinct object. A banjiki, on the other hand, is similar to a {{ill|Stone circle|en|Stone circle|qid=Q1935728|lt=Stone circle|1=Stone circle}}, an ancient ruin made of stones arranged in a ring, and serves as a boundary that marks the divine realm and preserves the sacredness of the site.
Some of the megaliths along the {{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|qid=Q749827|lt=Kaido|1=Kaidō}} have {{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|en|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|qid=Q17004306|lt=Buddhism|1=Hayagriva (Buddhism)}} carved on them, and some have {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|qid=Q44342|lt=en:Legend|1=Legend}}s attached to them, such as the famous samurai who connected {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|qid=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}s. There is a research group (Iwakura Gakkai) that claims that the belief in rock formations and megaliths, including these, can be traced back to the [[Jōmon period]], and that there are also artificially arranged rock formations, and that their arrangement represents certain figures, directions, or the shape of the {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|qid=Q8928|lt=Constellation|1=Constellation}}. In contrast to this view, the Iwakura Society points out that it was not until the [[Kofun period]] that the Iwakura ritual began.<ref>Tsutomu Tokieda, Archaeological Study of Mountain Sacred Sites, Yusankaku Inc. 2018</ref> In addition, there is criticism of the theory that megaliths are artifacts.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.mars.dti.ne.jp/~techno/column/asizuri.htm |script-title=ja:足摺岬縄文灯台騒動・最後のまとめ |website=www.mars.dti.ne.jp |date=1998 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210216013430/http://www.mars.dti.ne.jp/~techno/column/asizuri.htm |archive-date=16 February 2021 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== {{transliteration|ja|Iwasaka}} === An {{Nihongo||磐境|iwasaka}} is a stone altar or mound erected as a {{transliteration|ja|yorishiro}} to call a {{transliteration|ja|kami}} for worship.<ref name="iwas"/> The concepts of {{transliteration|ja|iwasaka}} and {{transliteration|ja|iwakura}} are so close that some suggest the two words are in fact synonymous.<ref name="iwas">Sugiyama, "Iwasaka"</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Hitsuishi, gaikan-3.jpg|{{ill|Hitsuishi ritual site|ja|櫃石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitsuishi ritual site|qq=}} File:Yagyu-Yamaguchi-jinja, Tateiwa-jinja, iwakura.jpg|Tachiban Shrine in the precincts of Yashifun Yamaguchi Shrine (Nara City, Nara Prefecture) File:Yoki-tenman-jinja Shrine - Iwakura.jpg|{{ill|Yoki Tenman Shrine|lt=Yoki Tenman Shrine|lt=Yoiki Tenma Shrine|ja|與喜天満神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoiki Tenma Shrine|qq=}} (Sakurai City, Nara Prefecture) File:Omiwa-jinja Iwakura.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} (Sakurai City, Nara Prefecture) File:Meotoiwa Site (Matsue), gaikan.jpg|{{ill|Meoto Iwa Ruins|ja|女夫岩遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Iwa Ruins|qq=}} File:Takashima (Okayama, Okayama), iwakura.jpg|Rocks on {{ill|Taka Island|ja|高島 (岡山市南区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka Island|qq=}} </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Kōgoishi|en|Kōgoishi|qid=Q6455119|lt=Kamagoishi|1=Kōgoishi}} * [[Himorogi]] * [[Utaki]] * {{ill|Megalith|en|Megalith|qid=Q164240|lt=Megalith|1=Megalith}} * {{ill|Fujizuka|en|Fujizuka|qid=Q5507555|lt=Fujizuka|1=Fujizuka}} * [[Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|Okinoshima]] * {{ill|Crystal healing|en|Crystal healing|qid=Q2071582|lt=Crystal healing|1=Crystal healing}} * {{ill|Cinnabar|en|Cinnabar|qid=Q104614|lt=Cinnabar|1=Cinnabar}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://iwakura.main.jp/index.html イワクラ（磐座）学会]
